caption:
🇯🇵👨🏼‍🍳 Check out Kappabashi Street aka “Kitchen Town” next time you’re in Tokyo.

#tokyo #japan #cooking
transcript: Whether you are a home cook, line cook, chef, or general food lover, you can't go to Tokyo without stopping at this place. This is Kapabashi Street in Tokyo, Japan, and if you cook, you must make the pilgrimage here at least once in your life. It's an entire neighborhood dedicated to cookware for both the home and professional kitchen, so there's a little something for all cooks across the board. You can start by popping into one of the dozens of Japanese knife shops. Some of them are super high-end and even offer custom engraving, but others offer budget options. I mean, I bought my first Japanese knife here about seven years ago, and it's still kicking today. High-quality stuff. Then, of course, there are the places that sell plates, bowls and cups, chopstick, chopstick holders. Basically, all the cute stuff you need to make your crib feel like a ramen shop. And for all my industry people out there, this street is heaven, or hell, because you're gonna drop bands. So make sure to buy a cheap suitcase to check because you're gonna need it. I'm talking specialty oyokodon pans, Japanese hammered pots, copper pans for Japanese omelets, horsehair brushes for pastry, yakumi pans for mise en place, the whole nine. However, this is probably the most unique and distinctly Japanese shop in the neighborhood. It's really popular for restaurants to display their menu items as little figurines outside the front door to entice customers to come eat. So people here have become really, really good at making hyper-realistic, basically action figures of your favorite Japanese dishes. I kind of want an entire plate of curry. Is that bad? In my opinion, it's a must-stop, and if you're looking for that distinctly Japanese gift for your favorite cook back home, or maybe a little piece of faux otoro for your desk, this is the place to cop it. I won't lie, I was dangerously close to buying the life-size ramen bowl with the floating chopsticks, but the waifu hit the brakes on that one, so next time. After draining my wife and I's joint bank account, I ended the day with like four bags of stuff for myself and, you know, souvenirs for others. So if you want to see a haul video, let me know in the comments. We can dig into it.

It's an entire neighborhood dedicated to cookware for both the home and professional kitchen, so there's a little something for all cooks across the board.
You can start by popping into one of the dozens of Japanese knife shops.
Some of them are super high-end and even offer custom engraving, but others offer budget options.
I mean, I bought my first Japanese knife here about seven years ago, and it's still kicking today. High-quality stuff.
Then, of course, there are the places that sell plates, bowls and cups, chopstick, chopstick holders.
Basically, all the cute stuff you need to make your crib feel like a ramen shop.

I'm talking specialty oyokodon pans, Japanese hammered pots, copper pans for Japanese omelets, horsehair brushes for pastry, yakumi pans for mise en place, the whole nine.
However, this is probably the most unique and distinctly Japanese shop in the neighborhood.
It's really popular for restaurants to display their menu items as little figurines outside the front door to entice customers to come eat.
